blocks.body: Non-defining Relative Clauses shows you how to add extra information about a noun that is already clear from the context.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"Non-defining relative clauses add extra information about a person, thing, or place that is already clear in the context.","rules":[{"label":"Extra, not essential","color":"emerald","text":"These clauses add a detail, but they do not identify the noun.","example":"My sister, who lives in Warsaw, is visiting tomorrow."},{"label":"Commas matter","color":"blue","text":"A non-defining clause is normally marked off by commas.","example":"This laptop, which I bought last year, still works well."},{"label":"That is unusual here","color":"amber","text":"In standard learner English, that is not usually used in non-defining clauses.","example":"My car, which is ten years old, still runs well."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. When we use a non-defining clause
blocks.body:
A non-defining clause works when the noun is already clear enough.

  • Mr Evans, who teaches chemistry, is absent today. - we already know who Mr Evans is.
  • Kyiv, where she studied, changed her life. - the name of the city is already specific.

The clause simply adds a comment or background detail.

blocks.heading: 2. The usual form
blocks.body:
• who - for people: My uncle, who works in Canada, is coming soon.
  • which - for things or whole situations: The meeting, which lasted two hours, was useful.
  • whose - for possession: Anna, whose brother is a doctor, moved to Poznan.
  • where - for places: Paris, where we met, still feels special.

In this type of clause, that is usually avoided in neutral learner style.

blocks.heading: 3. How to read the commas
blocks.body:
The commas show that the information is not needed to identify the noun.

  • My brother, who lives abroad, calls every Sunday. - I already have one specific brother in mind, and the clause just adds a fact.
  • The report, which arrived this morning, is on your desk. - we already know which report we mean.

In speech, you often hear a short pause before and after this clause.

blocks.heading: 4. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: leaving out the commas. In a non-defining clause, they are almost always needed.
  • Mistake: using that in the same way as in a defining clause. For learner clarity, use who or which instead.
  • Mistake: using a non-defining clause when the information actually identifies the noun. If the noun is unclear without the clause, you need a defining clause.

blocks.heading: 5. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• A non-defining relative clause gives extra information.
  • These clauses are separated by commas.
  • In basic learner style, it is better not to use that here.
  • If the noun is already clear without the clause, a non-defining clause fits well.
